sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check state
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    center = (sum(rows)/len(rows), sum(cols)/len(cols))
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N', 'c', 'G', 'b', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}, center={center}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, now I see:
- id=8: W, 16px, at (37.0, 33.5) - white block grew to 16px, carrying the bottom square
- id=9: b, 4px, at (37.5, 33.5) - the carried blue square
- id=7: N, 12px, at (33.0, 33.5) - green block (player)
- id=2: c, 12px, at (25.5, 33.5) - the G square turned back to c!
- id=1: c, 12px, at (25.5, 13.5) - left square still c
So when I picked up the bottom square, the previously dropped G square at (25.5, 33.5) turned back to c. This confirms that there's only ONE G state at a time, and picking up a new square causes the previous G to revert.
This changes my understanding. The mechanic seems to be:
1. SPACE picks up adjacent blue square (white grows to 16px)
2. SPACE drops the carried square as G at current position (white shrinks to 4px)
3. When you pick up a new square, any previously dropped G squares revert to c
4. The G state is only permanent if you don't pick up any more squares
So the goal can't be to have all 3 squares be G simultaneously, since picking up a new square reverts the previous ones.
Wait, but maybe the goal IS to drop the last square as G, and the previous G squares don't matter. Or maybe the goal is to drop all 3 squares at the SAME location (stacking them), and the final state has all 3 as G.
Let me try a different approach. Let me drop the bottom square at the SAME location as the previous G square (25.5, 33.5), and see if they stack.
Actually, looking at the current state, the G square at (25.5, 33.5) is now c. So if I drop the bottom square there, it'll become G, but the previous one is already c.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to:
1. Pick up all 3 squares
2. Stack them at the bar location
3. The final stacked square is G
But the white block can only carry one square at a time (16px). So I can't pick up all 3 at once.
Wait, maybe the white block can carry multiple squares. Let me check: when I picked up the bottom square, the white block grew to 16px. If I pick up another square while carrying one, does the white block grow further?
Let me try: I'm currently carrying the bottom square (white at 16px). Let me go to the left square and try to pick it up too.
Actually, the left square is at (25.5, 13.5), and I'm at (33.0, 33.5). That's far. Let me first drop the bottom square at the bar, then go get the left square.
Wait, but dropping the bottom square will make it G, and then picking up the left square will make it c again. So the final state would only have the left square as G.
Unless... the goal is to have the LAST dropped square be at a specific location (like the bar), and the previous G squares don't matter.
Let me try dropping the bottom square at the bar, then going to get the left square and dropping it at the bar too. If the goal is to have the final G square at the bar, this might work.
Actually, let me reconsider. The bar is at (29.5, 33.5). The player is at (33.0, 33.5). The carried square is at (37.5, 33.5). Let me drop the bottom square at the bar first.
To drop at the bar, I need to move to (29.5, 33.5). Player is at (33.0, 33.5), so LEFT 1 should get me there.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Carrying bottom square (W=16px). Previous G square reverted to c. Only one G state exists at a time. Let me drop at bar, then collect left square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Drop bottom square at bar, then go get left square
# Player at (33.0, 33.5), bar at (29.5, 33.5)
# LEFT 1 to get to bar, SPACE to drop
action(['LEFT', 'SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
